1	name: Nightly upstream merge
2	
3	# The ongoing half of the architecture. Nightly: if upstream superset published a
4	# newer desktop-v* tag than .fork/upstream-baseline.txt, merge that tag into the
5	# fork. git's 3-way merge handles the non-conflicting majority deterministically;
6	# Opus (--effort high) resolves ONLY the conflicted files, preserving every
7	# feature. Then the FEATURES.md marker gate runs, then the dependency gates, then
8	# (MERGE-SEMANTIC-GATE) an Opus review of the WHOLE merged delta — clean or
9	# conflicted — against the fork feature manifest (a cleanly-merging upstream hunk
10	# can semantically break a fork feature with zero conflicts; v1.14.2 dropped the
11	# sidebar snooze/bin fields that way). Only then the deterministic ARM64 build.
12	# On a clean merge + green build the build publishes a full Release and we
13	# fast-forward the baseline. Any failure (unresolvable conflict, lost marker,
14	# semantic breakage verdict, build failure) hard-aborts and leaves the baseline
15	# untouched -> recovery loop.
16	#
17	# AI cost scales with the UPSTREAM delta per release, NOT with how large the fork
18	# grows. The build itself stays 100% AI-free.
19	#
20	# NOTE: schedule only fires from the DEFAULT branch, so this is dormant until the
21	# fork-baseline branch is promoted to main.
22	
23	on:
24	  schedule:
25	    - cron: '0 5 * * *' # 05:00 UTC daily
26	  workflow_dispatch:
27	    inputs:
28	      force_tag:
29	        description: 'Merge a specific upstream desktop-v* tag (blank = latest)'
30	        required: false
31	        default: ''
32	
33	permissions:
34	  contents: write
35	
36	jobs:
37	  merge:
38	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
39	    outputs:
40	      should_build: ${{ steps.do.outputs.should_build }}
41	      newtag: ${{ steps.do.outputs.newtag }}
42	      version: ${{ steps.do.outputs.version }}
43	      candidate: ${{ steps.do.outputs.candidate }}
44	    env:
45	      CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN }}
46	      DISABLE_NON_ESSENTIAL_MODEL_CALLS: '1'
47	      CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_NONESSENTIAL_TRAFFIC: '1'
48	      # Conflict resolution can touch large files; give Opus headroom.
49	      CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_OUTPUT_TOKENS: '128000'
50	    steps:
51	      - name: Checkout fork (full history)
52	        uses: actions/checkout@v5
53	        with:
54	          fetch-depth: 0
55	
56	      - name: Setup Node.js
57	        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
58	        with:
59	          node-version: '22'
60	
61	      # (LOCK-REGEN) Lock regeneration below needs bun; pin the EXACT version
62	      # the build uses so the lock we commit is byte-identical to what the
63	      # frozen build install consumes.
64	      - name: Setup Bun (match the build's pinned version)
65	        uses: oven-sh/setup-bun@v2
66	        with:
67	          bun-version: '1.3.14'
68	
69	      - name: Merge upstream + resolve + gate
70	        id: do
71	        shell: bash
72	        run: |
73	          set -euo pipefail
74	          git config user.name "superset-arm64-bot"
75	          git config user.email "actions@users.noreply.github.com"
76	
77	          # --- detect the upstream tag to merge --------------------------------
78	          git remote add upstream https://github.com/superset-sh/superset.git 2>/dev/null || true
79	          # Fetch upstream's desktop-v* tags into an ISOLATED ref namespace.
80	          # Our OWN one-version release tags live in refs/tags/desktop-v<version>
81	          # (gh release create makes them at our build commit). A plain fetch into
82	          # refs/tags/ collides with every released version ("would clobber existing
83	          # tag" -> non-zero fetch -> set -e abort). The isolated namespace + --force
84	          # keeps upstream's source tags cleanly separate from our release tags.
85	          git fetch --no-recurse-submodules --force upstream \
86	            'refs/tags/desktop-v*:refs/upstream-tags/desktop-v*'
87	          FORCE='${{ github.event.inputs.force_tag }}'
88	          if [ -n "$FORCE" ]; then
89	            NEWTAG="$FORCE"
90	          else
91	            LATEST=$(git for-each-ref --format='%(refname:lstrip=2)' 'refs/upstream-tags/desktop-v*' | sed -n 's/^desktop-v\([0-9][0-9.]*\)$/\1/p' | sort -V | tail -1)
92	            NEWTAG="desktop-v${LATEST}"
93	          fi
94	          BASE=$(tr -d '[:space:]' < .fork/upstream-baseline.txt)
95	          echo "baseline=$BASE  latest_upstream=$NEWTAG"
96	          if [ "$NEWTAG" = "$BASE" ]; then
97	            echo "Already at $BASE — nothing to merge."
98	            echo "should_build=false" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
99	            exit 0
100	          fi
101	          if ! git rev-parse -q --verify "refs/upstream-tags/$NEWTAG^{commit}" >/dev/null; then
102	            echo "::error::upstream tag $NEWTAG not found"; exit 1
103	          fi
104	
105	          # --- candidate branch + merge ---------------------------------------
106	          CAND="nightly/${NEWTAG}"
107	          git switch -c "$CAND"
108	          echo "Merging $NEWTAG into the fork (no auto-commit)…"
109	          set +e
110	          git merge --no-commit --no-ff "refs/upstream-tags/$NEWTAG^{commit}"
111	          set -e
112	
113	          # (WORKFLOW-FORK-OWNED) .github/workflows is FORK-OWNED: the fork
114	          # carries ZERO upstream workflow files (upstream CI targets upstream's
115	          # infra/secrets and is meaningless here), and the Actions GITHUB_TOKEN
116	          # can NEVER push a .github/workflows/* change — GitHub rejects both
117	          # the candidate push and the main fast-forward with "refusing to allow
118	          # a GitHub App to create or update workflow … without `workflows`
119	          # permission" (desktop-v1.14.3 deploy-relay.yml incident: the merge,
120	          # gates and review all passed, then the push died). Restore our exact
121	          # workflow set BEFORE conflict detection: upstream-ADDED files are
122	          # dropped from index+worktree (--no-overlay), upstream edits to files
123	          # we long deleted resolve as ours, and the AI resolver never sees a
124	          # workflow path it could resurrect.
125	          git checkout --no-overlay HEAD -- .github/workflows
126	
127	          CONFLICTS=$(git diff --name-only --diff-filter=U || true)
128	          if [ -n "$CONFLICTS" ]; then
129	            echo "::group::conflicts to resolve"; echo "$CONFLICTS"; echo "::endgroup::"
130	            npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
131	            PROMPT="IMPORTANT: Do NOT enter plan mode. You are inside a git repository mid-merge: upstream superset tag ${NEWTAG} is being merged into our Windows-ARM64 fork. ONLY the files listed by 'git diff --name-only --diff-filter=U' are conflicted. Resolve EVERY conflicted file: combine upstream's change with our fork's feature so BOTH intents survive and our feature keeps working; where upstream deliberately removed or refactored something we had only lightly touched, follow upstream. Do NOT edit any non-conflicted file. Do NOT weaken or remove any feature marker tracked in FEATURES.md. After resolving each file run 'git add <file>'. Do NOT commit. Do NOT run any other git command. When every conflict is resolved and staged, stop. Output nothing."
132	            attempt=1
133	            while [ $attempt -le 3 ]; do
134	              echo "::group::claude resolve attempt $attempt"
135	              claude --dangerously-skip-permissions --model opus --effort high -p "$PROMPT" || true
136	              echo "::endgroup::"
137	              if [ -z "$(git diff --name-only --diff-filter=U || true)" ]; then break; fi
138	              attempt=$((attempt+1))
139	            done
140	            REMAIN=$(git diff --name-only --diff-filter=U || true)
141	            if [ -n "$REMAIN" ]; then
142	              echo "::error::Opus could not resolve all conflicts for $NEWTAG — recovery: fix locally and re-baseline. Remaining:"; echo "$REMAIN"
143	              exit 1
144	            fi
145	          else
146	            echo "Clean merge — no conflicts."
147	          fi
148

1	# Fork feature manifest
2	
3	This file is the source of truth for **what our fork adds on top of upstream
4	superset**. The nightly upstream-merge workflow greps every marker below against
5	the merged tree; **if any marker is missing the merge is rejected** (a feature
6	was dropped or an upstream change clobbered it) and the build hard-aborts into
7	the recovery loop. Markers are deliberately distinctive identifiers that only
8	exist because of our feature code.
9	
10	When you add a new custom feature, add a marker row here in the same commit.
11	When upstream **deprecates/removes** a feature we mirrored, remove its marker row
12	in the merge that drops it (the only legitimate way a marker leaves this list).
13	
14	## Features
15	
16	| Feature | What it is | Marker |
17	|---|---|---|
18	| Native Windows ARM64 packaging | one-click NSIS installer; ARM64 node-pty/libsql/tokenizers | `npm:@lydell/node-pty`, `bun-windows-arm64` |
19	| Window controls | native `titleBarOverlay` is the sole min/max/close set on Windows | `titleBarOverlay` |
20	| Windows behaviour fixes | child-process patch, cmd.exe fallback, force-foreground, watchdog, WebGL recovery, fast startup | `windows-child-process` |
21	| Agent status dots (Claude + Codex) | per-terminal dot + workspace rollup; host-service POST + JSONL watcher | `pane-map-hook`, `StatusIndicator` |
22	| Claude Stop unhooked from notify.sh | upstream's raw Stop passthrough must never race superset-notify.py (it wiped the blue dot + yellow-hold) | `CLAUDE-STOP-UNHOOKED` |
23	| Compaction shows working | Claude context compaction (manual /compact + auto) drives the yellow dot via PreCompact / SessionStart(source=compact) | `(COMPACT-YELLOW)` |
24	| Team/workflow work shows working | background_tasks[] entry types split the post-turn dot: agent-type work (teammate/subagent/workflow) holds yellow; shell-only goes blue | `(TEAM-YELLOW)` |
25	| Codex-companion job holds the dot | detached codex worker is invisible to background_tasks[]; an active (pid-alive) codex job for the session holds yellow incl. through StopFailure AND the JSONL watcher's stream-idle/API-abort + interrupt self-heal (System 1 mirrors the same check via `codexJobActive` in agent-jsonl-watcher.ts, so a codex-only session can't false-green when the Claude API drops and no StopFailure hook fires) | `_codex_job_active` |
26	| Manual compact restores shell-blue | a manual /compact ending while only a background shell runs restores the BackgroundRunning blue (turn-end snapshot marker) instead of false-greening; the JSONL-watcher re-attach after compaction must not wipe that blue | `_shellbg_marker_path`, `(BLUE-SPECTATOR)` |
27	| ws native modules off | host-service `ws` must use pure-JS mask/unmask — a broken packaged bufferutil wedges WS receivers and kills ALL terminal keyboard input | `WS_NO_BUFFER_UTIL` |
28	| Shell-running blue dot | OSC 133 C/D command-running detection | `scanForOsc133Cd` |
29	| Non-git / multi-repo workspaces | open a non-git folder as a plain workspace | `resolveNonGitFolder` |
30	| Multi-repo branch workspaces | "Open from multi-folder" groups N git repos; "+" fans the same branch out as a worktree per repo under one container workspace | `readMultiRepoConfig`, `createMultiRepoWorkspaceFlow` |
31	| Multi-repo member management | Project Settings "Repositories" section adds/removes member repos of a multi-repo project. Add is lazy (included in NEW branch workspaces only — cleanup already tolerates a missing member worktree); remove force-removes that repo's worktrees from every existing branch container (confirm dialog; repo + branches kept) BEFORE the config rewrite so no orphan worktrees/registrations strand. Min 2 members enforced | `addMultiRepoMember`, `MultiRepoMembersSection` |
32	| Workspace branch label | the open workspace page names its branch top-right in the tab bar (click copies) — the only branch surface a non-git multi-repo container has | `WorkspaceBranchLabel` |
33	| Workspace-create failure toast | every workspace/worktree create failure raises a toast with the actual reason (e.g. a GitHub/network timeout) from the one `recordFailure` chokepoint — so a transient failure no longer reads as a silent "stuck creating → reload" | `(CREATE-FAIL-TOAST)` |
34	| Thread snooze / archive | per-thread timed Snooze + sticky Archive in the sidebar | `getWorkspaceSidebarBucket`, `APP_LAUNCH_ID`, `DashboardSidebarStateSection` |
35	| Sidebar row projection carries the fork state fields | the host-owned sidebar row projection (`rawSidebarWorkspaces` in `useDashboardSidebarData`) must carry `snoozeUntil`/`snoozeLaunchId`/`archivedAt`/`completedAt`/`deletedAt` from `v2WorkspaceLocalState` — the v1.14.2 upstream merge rebuilt that mapping and silently dropped them, which broke snooze (rows never left the active lane), emptied the Recycle Bin, vanished archived masters, and resurfaced completed threads under Archived | `(SIDEBAR-STATE-PROJECTION)` |
36	| Master rows snoozeable | `snoozeWorkspace` inserts a pre-snoozed local-state row for an auto-included main that has none yet (mirrors `archiveWorkspace`) — Snooze on a master row used to silently no-op | `(SNOOZE-MAIN)` |
37	| Sticky project removal | "Remove project from sidebar" tombstones EVERY workspace of the project incl. mains, and passive workspace route mounts (session restore, kanban split) use `placeWorkspaceFromPassiveMount`, which never re-inserts the project's sidebar row and never resurrects an existing hidden row — a removed project can only come back via an EXPLICIT open (Workspaces page, project setup/import) | `(REMOVE-STICKY)` |
38	| Case-insensitive repo-path dedupe | host-service `project.findByPath` / import-collision checks compare `projects.repoPath` case-insensitively on win32/darwin (case-insensitive filesystems) — a case-only folder rename used to miss the existing row and mint a duplicate project (cloud slug conflict suffixed `-2` instead of reusing) | `(PATH-CI-DEDUPE)` |
39	| Merge semantic review gate | every nightly upstream merge — clean or conflicted — is reviewed by Opus against this manifest BEFORE it may commit/build/publish: a cleanly-merging upstream hunk that semantically breaks a fork feature (the v1.14.2 sidebar-projection incident) is caught here, not in production. Fail closed: no parsable verdict, a BREAKAGE verdict, or a review run that modified the tree all hard-abort with the baseline untouched | `(MERGE-SEMANTIC-GATE)` |
40	| Sidebar hover-freeze | rows never re-sort while the pointer is over the project list (order applies on leave) | `(HOVER-FREEZE)` |
41	| Terminal links | plain click copies a URL/path, Ctrl/Cmd+click opens; `.html` paths open in Chrome (OS default fallback) | `useLinkClickHint`, `openHtmlInBrowser` |
42	| Agent-hook bash-wrap | Gemini/Cursor `.sh` hooks run via Git-for-Windows bash | `agent-wrappers` |
43	| Kanban board | device-local board mirroring branches + Queued column | `v2KanbanCards`, `KANBAN_QUEUE_COLUMN_ID` |
44	| Kanban Completed column | fixed FINAL column: dropping a card stamps an editable completed date and hides the thread from the sidebar ENTIRELY (drag out un-completes/restores); per-column date filter (all / last calendar month / custom range) for work-done reports; completed cards survive branch deletion as frozen records; main cards can't complete | `KANBAN_COMPLETED_COLUMN_ID` |
45	| Kanban append-only backup | daily write-once JSON snapshot of the board; code can never delete/overwrite one | `writeKanbanBackup` |
46	| Kanban sidebar button toggles | sidebar Kanban press: anywhere → full-screen board; part-screen split → full-screen (closing then reopens that workspace full size); full-screen → close back to the remembered previous page (fallback Workspaces list) | `(KANBAN-TOGGLE)` |
47	| Subagent tool events never stomp the red | a PostToolUse whose payload carries `agent_id` (ran inside a subagent) maps to the red-respecting SubagentActive — background agents' tool completions must not clear a pending AskUserQuestion/permission red; only a main-loop completion does. SubagentStart likewise | `(SUBTOOL-RED)` |

... [89 lines truncated] ...

20	  apply, no arch-fixup. `.fork/upstream-baseline.txt` records the upstream tag the
21	  baseline sits on.
22	- **Nightly (minimal AI).** `.github/workflows/nightly-merge.yml` runs nightly: if
23	  upstream published a newer `desktop-v*` tag, it `git merge`s it in. Git resolves
24	  the non-conflicting majority deterministically; an **Opus** model (`--effort
25	  high`) resolves **only the conflicted files**, preserving every feature. AI cost
26	  scales with the **upstream delta per release**, NOT with how large the fork grows.
27	- **Gates.** After the merge: every marker in `FEATURES.md` must still be present
28	  (else a feature was dropped → reject), the dependency gates run, then
29	  `(MERGE-SEMANTIC-GATE)`: Opus reviews the WHOLE merged delta — clean or
30	  conflicted — against `FEATURES.md` for semantic feature breakage (fail closed:
31	  no verdict / a BREAKAGE verdict / a review that edited the tree all abort),
32	  then the deterministic build must go green.
33	  On a clean merge + green build the nightly **auto-publishes the single
34	  `desktop-v<version>` Release** (rebuilt in place — see One-version below) and
35	  advances the baseline. Any failure (unresolvable conflict, lost marker,
36	  breakage verdict, build failure) **hard-aborts** and leaves the baseline
37	  untouched → recovery.
38	- **Recovery.** When a nightly merge can't be done cleanly, fix it locally with the
39	  maintainer, rebuild, re-freeze the baseline; the nightly then resumes merging only
40	  *new* upstream changes from that point.
41	
42	## Fork non-negotiables
43	
44	- **Whole feature set or fail loud.** Every `FEATURES.md` marker survives a merge or
45	  the build aborts. Never ship a partial fork.
...
43	  electron-vite/esbuild; type/format errors don't fail it (the tree carries
44	  accepted type debt). The exception is `(REFERR-GATE)`
45	  (`scripts/check-dangerous-diagnostics.mjs`, run by the build workflow, and
46	  therefore by every nightly): cannot-find-name tsc diagnostics
47	  (TS2304/2552-class) DO fail the build — bare identifiers that throw
48	  ReferenceError at runtime, the exact damage a cleanly-merging upstream hunk
49	  leaves in fork code the AI resolver never sees (v1.14.0 layout.tsx
50	  incident). Validate + e2e locally before relying on a release.
51	- **AI only in the nightly merge.** The build is AI-free. The nightly needs
52	  `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN` for the conflict resolver AND the mandatory
53	  `(MERGE-SEMANTIC-GATE)` review of every merge; if rate-limited it aborts rather
54	  than ship a half-merged or unreviewed fork.
55	- **One version, ever.** Exactly one GitHub Release per upstream version, tagged
56	  `desktop-v<version>`, **rebuilt in place** (delete + recreate so the tag always
57	  points at the latest build). NO `-beta`, NO prerelease, NO separate release/beta
58	  split — there is just *the* `desktop-v<version>` build.
59	
60	## Fork features (high-level + UX, for a full rebuild)
61	
62	See `FEATURES.md` for the marker manifest. In brief:
...
217	  `(WORKFLOW-FORK-OWNED)`. Never remove that step; add an upstream workflow only
218	  by deliberate local commit with a user token (which has `workflow` scope).
219	
220	## Fork limitations (accepted)
221	
222	- Unsigned → SmartScreen warns.
223	- No full build-time type/test gate (only the narrow `(REFERR-GATE)`
224	  cannot-find-name check) — validate + e2e locally.
225	- An auto-merged nightly can publish a Release unattended, gated by the
226	  feature-marker check + `(MERGE-SEMANTIC-GATE)` (Opus reviews every merged
227	  delta for fork-feature breakage) + `(REFERR-GATE)` + the esbuild build — the
228	  AI review shrinks but does not eliminate the semantically-wrong-but-compiling
229	  window. e2e-test before relying on a release.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: C:\Users\khair\.claude\skills\call-another-llm

<purpose>
Send a prompt to another LLM agent and get its response saved to a file.
Three separate scripts, one per LLM. Each runs the CLI in non-interactive mode and writes the raw response to tmp/llm-responses/.
</purpose>

<constraints>
- Each script runs the target LLM in non-interactive mode with auto-approve.
- Fail fast if the target CLI is not installed or returns a non-zero exit code.
- Output files contain only the raw response — no metadata.
- All output goes to `tmp/llm-responses/` with the pattern `<llm>-<label>-<timestamp>.md`.
</constraints>

<scripts>
- `scripts/call_codex.py` — calls Codex CLI
- `scripts/call_claude.py` — calls Claude Code CLI
- `scripts/call_gemini.py` — calls Gemini CLI
</scripts>

<inputs>

**All scripts share:**
  - positional: `prompt` (required, except when using `--list-models`)
  - `--label`: optional label for the output filename (default: llm name)

**Codex-specific flags (`call_codex.py`):**
  - `-m` / `--model`: model to use (e.g. `gpt-5.4`, `gpt-5.3-codex-spark`)
  - `--effort`: reasoning effort level — `none`, `minimal`, `low`, `medium`, `high`, `xhigh`
  - `--list-models`: print known models, the current default, and valid effort levels, then exit

When `--model` is omitted, Codex uses the default from `~/.codex/config.toml`.
When `--effort` is omitted, Codex uses the effort configured in `config.toml` (default: `high`).
</inputs>

<workflow>
1. If the user has not specified which LLM to call, use the AskUser tool with options (Codex, Claude, Gemini) to let the user choose. If the user wants all three, confirm that before running three parallel calls.
2. Write the prompt to a file first (see prompt-file-methodology below).
3. Run the chosen script(s) passing the file contents: `"$(cat tmp/llm-prompts/{filename})"`.
4. Read the output file path from stdout (OUTPUT_FILE=...).
5. Read the response file to use the result.
</workflow>

<prompt-file-methodology>
Always write prompts to a file before passing to CLI scripts. This makes prompts reviewable,
debuggable, and preservable as an audit trail.

**Directory:** `tmp/llm-prompts/` (create with `mkdir -p` if it doesn't exist)

**File naming:** `{agentname}_{3 random lowercase letters}_{unix timestamp}.txt`
- `agentname` = the agent role or label (e.g. `codex-reviewer`, `gemini-crosscheck`, `claude-summarizer`)
- 3 random lowercase letters for uniqueness
- Unix timestamp in seconds
- Example: `codex-reviewer_xfm_1743926400.txt`

**Steps:**
1. `mkdir -p tmp/llm-prompts`
2. Generate filename: `{agentname}_$(cat /dev/urandom | tr -dc 'a-z' | head -c3)_$(date +%s).txt`
3. Write the full prompt to that file.
4. Pass to CLI: `uv run .../call_codex.py --label "..." "$(cat tmp/llm-prompts/{filename})"`
</prompt-file-methodology>

<examples>
```bash
# List available Codex models and effort levels
uv run "C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/mk-skills/call-another-llm/scripts/call_codex.py" --list-models

# Codex with default model and effort
uv run "C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/mk-skills/call-another-llm/scripts/call_codex.py" "Explain the trade-offs of this approach" --label "review"

# Codex with specific model and effort
uv run "C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/mk-skills/call-another-llm/scripts/call_codex.py" "Review this code" -m gpt-5.3-codex-spark --effort low --label "quick-review"
uv run "C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/mk-skills/call-another-llm/scripts/call_codex.py" "Deep analysis of this architecture" -m gpt-5.4 --effort xhigh --label "deep-review"

# Claude and Gemini
uv run "C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/mk-skills/call-another-llm/scripts/call_claude.py" "Summarise this error log" --label "error-summary"
uv run "C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/mk-skills/call-another-llm/scripts/call_gemini.py" "What are the security implications?" --label "security"
```
</examples>

cd "/c/Users/khair/Code Projects/fork-superset-windows-arm64" && mkdir -p tmp/llm-prompts && f="tmp/llm-prompts/codex-nightly-advice_$(tr -dc 'a-z' < /dev/urandom | head -c3)_$(date +%s).txt" && { cat > "$f" <<'EOF'
You are advising on the CI of a vendored fork repo (khairm/superset-windows-arm64): full upstream source (superset-sh/superset) committed, fork features on top, nightly workflow merges each new upstream desktop-v* tag, AI resolves only conflicted files, gates run (feature-marker grep, dependency/lock gates, AI semantic review), then a deterministic Windows-ARM64 build publishes a Release and fast-forwards main. You have read access to the repo at the current working directory — read any file you want, e.g. .github/workflows/nightly-merge.yml, .github/workflows/build-arm64.yml, FEATURES.md, AGENTS.md (fork-maintenance section at top).

INCIDENT (2026-07-11, run 29144046328): upstream desktop-v1.14.3 added a NEW workflow file .github/workflows/deploy-relay.yml. The nightly merge went perfectly — conflicts resolved, all gates + semantic review passed, merge commit created — then the candidate-branch push was rejected:
  ! [remote rejected] nightly/desktop-v1.14.3 (refusing to allow a GitHub App to create or update workflow `.github/workflows/deploy-relay.yml` without `workflows` permission)
The Actions GITHUB_TOKEN (a GitHub App installation token) can never create/update .github/workflows/* files — no `workflows` permission scope is grantable to it. The advance job's fast-forward push of main would hit the same rejection. This was the first upstream workflow-file change since the fork was created; the fork's .github/workflows/ deliberately carries ONLY the fork's two workflows (build-arm64.yml, nightly-merge.yml) — all upstream CI files were dropped at fork creation (they target upstream's infra/secrets and would run+fail noisily in the fork).

FIX SHIPPED (commit 17cae2de9 on main): codify .github/workflows as fork-owned. In the merge job, immediately after `git merge --no-commit --no-ff <upstream-tag>` and BEFORE conflict detection (`git diff --name-only --diff-filter=U`), run:
  git checkout --no-overlay HEAD -- .github/workflows
Effect (validated by locally replaying the exact v1.14.3 merge): upstream-ADDED workflow files are removed from index+worktree; upstream edits to files we deleted long ago resolve as ours; the AI conflict resolver never sees a workflow path (so it cannot resurrect one); the conflict set for other files is unchanged. A FEATURES.md marker (WORKFLOW-FORK-OWNED) rooted at .github guards the step's presence; AGENTS.md documents the trap. Alternatives considered and rejected: SSH deploy key or PAT for the push (new credential to manage, and upstream CI files would then flow in and run/fail noisily in the fork forever).

YOUR TASK — independent expert review, be adversarial and concrete:
1. Validate or refute the root-cause analysis. Anything mischaracterized?
2. Critique the fix. Is `git checkout --no-overlay HEAD -- .github/workflows` mid-merge (unmerged index possible) fully sound? Edge cases: modify/delete conflicts on workflow paths, upstream renames into/out of .github/workflows, directory-vs-pathspec subtleties, git version behavior differences, interaction with `set -euo pipefail`, anything that could make the step fail or silently do the wrong thing on a future night.
3. Residual failure modes of the same class anywhere else in this pipeline: is there ANY other path by which CI pushes could be rejected for permissions (e.g. tag pushes in build-arm64.yml, gh release create/delete, the advance job, branch protection interactions)? Read the workflows and check.
4. Is dropping ALL upstream workflow changes forever the right policy for a vendored fork, versus the deploy-key alternative? Any hybrid you would prefer? Consider: upstream may someday move build-relevant logic INTO a workflow file (e.g. codegen invoked only from CI) — how would this fork notice it lost something?
5. Any additional cheap, deterministic guard you would add so this incident class (merge succeeds, push rejected → nightly silently dead until a human looks) fails earlier or louder.
Output: numbered findings with severity (BLOCKER/HIGH/MED/LOW/INFO) + a bottom-line verdict on whether the shipped fix is sufficient.
EOF
} && echo "$f" && wc -c "$f"

content.content:
1	## Findings
2	
3	1. **INFO — Root cause is correct, with one wording correction.**  
4	   The automatic Actions `GITHUB_TOKEN` is a GitHub App installation token, and its configurable workflow permissions do not include `workflows: write`; `contents: write` therefore cannot authorize these changes. Both the candidate push and later `main` push would reject the added YAML. However, “no GitHub App can have this permission” is too broad: a separately registered GitHub App can request `Workflows: write`. The limitation is specific to the built-in Actions token. [GitHub’s GITHUB_TOKEN documentation](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/concepts/security/github_token), [GitHub App permission documentation](https://docs.github.com/en/enterprise-cloud@latest/apps/creating-github-apps/registering-a-github-app/choosing-permissions-for-a-github-app).
5	
6	2. **INFO — The checkout command is sound for ordinary cases.**  
7	   [`git checkout --no-overlay HEAD -- .github/workflows`](</C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/fork-superset-windows-arm64/.github/workflows/nightly-merge.yml:125>) replaces that subtree in both index and worktree from `HEAD`. `--no-overlay` removes indexed files absent from `HEAD`. Consequently, upstream additions, deletions, add/add conflicts, and modify/delete conflicts inside the directory are correctly resolved to the fork’s version. Other unmerged paths do not prevent an explicit tree-ish/path checkout. This behavior has existed since Git 2.23, well below `ubuntu-latest`’s version. [Git checkout documentation](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-checkout.html).
8	
9	3. **HIGH — The adjacent merge command suppresses fatal errors.**  
10	   [`set +e; git merge …; set -e`](</C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/fork-superset-windows-arm64/.github/workflows/nightly-merge.yml:109>) accepts every nonzero result, not just conflict exit status. A fatal merge that leaves no conflicts could be reported as “Clean merge”; the workflow could then update `.fork/upstream-baseline.txt` and create a single-parent commit without actually merging upstream. Capture `merge_rc`, reject unexpected values, and require a valid `MERGE_HEAD` before continuing.
11	
12	4. **MED — Cross-boundary renames are not handled atomically.**  
13	   If upstream moves `scripts/x` into `.github/workflows/x`, the checkout deletes the destination but does not restore `scripts/x`, silently accepting the source deletion. A rename out of the directory can leave the restored old path plus the new outside path. The v1.14.3 replay proves the simple addition case, not this future invariant. Before restoration, inspect the full upstream-to-upstream diff with rename detection and fail for review when either side of an `R`/`C` crosses the workflow boundary.
14	
15	5. **MED — “The AI cannot resurrect one” is not enforced.**  
16	   Removing workflow paths from the conflict list means the resolver should not touch them, but Claude still has write access to the entire tree. There is no exact postcondition before [`git add -A`](</C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/fork-superset-windows-arm64/.github/workflows/nightly-merge.yml:261>). The marker gate only proves that `(WORKFLOW-FORK-OWNED)` text remains somewhere under `.github`; retaining the comment while removing or defeating the command passes. Immediately before `git add -A`, require an empty:
17	
18	   ```bash
19	   git status --porcelain=v1 --untracked-files=all -- .github/workflows
20	   ```
21	
22	   After committing, also assert that the merge’s first-parent diff contains no `.github/workflows/` path.
23	
24	6. **HIGH — Dropping upstream workflows creates a genuine audit blind spot.**  
25	   Keeping upstream YAML non-executable in this fork is the right policy, but erasing it before both conflict detection and semantic review means nobody reviews what was discarded. Upstream has previously delivered build-relevant behavior only through workflow changes:
26	
27	   - `70cc2a34d`: added `generate:icons` to the desktop build.
28	   - `40f59ec0a`: added `app-update.yml` verification.
29	   - `60093e1e0`: added a required desktop build environment variable.
30	
31	   I would keep the directory fork-owned but require explicit acknowledgement of every upstream workflow-tree change. Compute the full upstream-to-upstream rename-aware diff before restoration, preserve it in the job summary/artifact and semantic-review input, and fail closed until a committed acknowledgement records the reviewed upstream workflow tree OID. This catches future build logic that must be selectively ported without importing upstream CI wholesale.
32	
33	7. **BLOCKER — Release tags currently target the wrong commit.**  
34	   [`gh release create`](</C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/fork-superset-windows-arm64/.github/workflows/build-arm64.yml:300>) runs after deleting the tag but without `--target`. When the tag is absent, `gh` creates it from the default branch—not checked-out candidate `HEAD`. During nightly, `main` has not yet advanced. Thus the installer is built from the candidate while its release tag points to the old baseline. [GitHub CLI documentation](https://cli.github.com/manual/gh_release_create).
35	
36	   Repository evidence confirms this happened: local `desktop-v1.14.2` points at `13437b4a…`, whose desktop version is `1.14.0`; the verified merge `e376fbf7…` contains `1.14.2`. Use `--target "$(git rev-parse HEAD)"` and assert the resulting remote tag equals that SHA.
37	
38	   Additionally, the release/tag deletions at [lines 298–299](</C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/fork-superset-windows-arm64/.github/workflows/build-arm64.yml:298>) suppress every failure. A protected or stale tag can therefore survive and be silently reused. Suppress only the expected “not found” case and verify absence/identity explicitly.
39	
40	8. **HIGH — Concurrent runs can build and advance different commits.**  
41	   There is no workflow-level `concurrency`, and the candidate is passed around as mutable branch name `nightly/desktop-vX`. Overlapping scheduled/manual runs can delete and replace that branch between merge, build checkout, and [`advance` fetch](</C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/fork-superset-windows-arm64/.github/workflows/nightly-merge.yml:306>). Add a fixed concurrency group with `cancel-in-progress: false`, output the candidate SHA, and build/advance only that immutable SHA with equality assertions.
42	
43	9. **MED — Other permission rejection paths remain.**  
44	   `contents: write` is sufficient for ordinary branch/tag/release operations, but it does not bypass:
45	
46	   - Branch protection or rulesets on `main` or `nightly/*`.
47	   - Required PRs, signed commits, status checks, or linear history.
48	   - Tag protection/rulesets or immutable releases.
49	
50	   The [`main` push](</C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/fork-superset-windows-arm64/.github/workflows/nightly-merge.yml:308>) occurs after the public release is created, so rejection leaves a published release with an unadvanced baseline. The following candidate deletion can also fail after `main` has already advanced, making a successful rollout appear failed. [Protected branch documentation](https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/configuring-branches-and-merges-in-your-repository/managing-protected-branches/about-protected-branches), [ruleset documentation](https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/configuring-branches-and-merges-in-your-repository/managing-rulesets/available-rules-for-rulesets).
51	
52	10. **MED — Failure needs a durable alert.**  
53	    Add an always-run failure job that creates or updates one deduplicated “nightly merge blocked” issue containing the tag, failed phase, and run URL. That requires `issues: write`. A separate external watchdog is needed to detect cases where the workflow never starts because it is disabled or invalid.
54	
55	## Bottom-line verdict
56	
57	The shipped checkout command is sufficient for the exact v1.14.3 workflow-addition incident and normal workflow-path conflicts. Making `.github/workflows` fork-owned is preferable to introducing a PAT/deploy key merely to import unwanted upstream CI.
58	
59	The pipeline as shipped is not yet sufficient overall. Before the next automatic publication, I would treat the wrong release-tag target as a blocker, then add merge-result validation, immutable candidate SHA/concurrency, and an exact no-workflow-delta assertion. For long-term vendored-fork safety, upstream workflow changes also need a separate fail-closed audit rather than being silently erased.

file.content:
## Findings

1. **INFO — Root cause is correct, with one wording correction.**  
   The automatic Actions `GITHUB_TOKEN` is a GitHub App installation token, and its configurable workflow permissions do not include `workflows: write`; `contents: write` therefore cannot authorize these changes. Both the candidate push and later `main` push would reject the added YAML. However, “no GitHub App can have this permission” is too broad: a separately registered GitHub App can request `Workflows: write`. The limitation is specific to the built-in Actions token. [GitHub’s GITHUB_TOKEN documentation](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/concepts/security/github_token), [GitHub App permission documentation](https://docs.github.com/en/enterprise-cloud@latest/apps/creating-github-apps/registering-a-github-app/choosing-permissions-for-a-github-app).

2. **INFO — The checkout command is sound for ordinary cases.**  
   [`git checkout --no-overlay HEAD -- .github/workflows`](</C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/fork-superset-windows-arm64/.github/workflows/nightly-merge.yml:125>) replaces that subtree in both index and worktree from `HEAD`. `--no-overlay` removes indexed files absent from `HEAD`. Consequently, upstream additions, deletions, add/add conflicts, and modify/delete conflicts inside the directory are correctly resolved to the fork’s version. Other unmerged paths do not prevent an explicit tree-ish/path checkout. This behavior has existed since Git 2.23, well below `ubuntu-latest`’s version. [Git checkout documentation](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-checkout.html).

3. **HIGH — The adjacent merge command suppresses fatal errors.**  
   [`set +e; git merge …; set -e`](</C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/fork-superset-windows-arm64/.github/workflows/nightly-merge.yml:109>) accepts every nonzero result, not just conflict exit status. A fatal merge that leaves no conflicts could be reported as “Clean merge”; the workflow could then update `.fork/upstream-baseline.txt` and create a single-parent commit without actually merging upstream. Capture `merge_rc`, reject unexpected values, and require a valid `MERGE_HEAD` before continuing.

4. **MED — Cross-boundary renames are not handled atomically.**  
   If upstream moves `scripts/x` into `.github/workflows/x`, the checkout deletes the destination but does not restore `scripts/x`, silently accepting the source deletion. A rename out of the directory can leave the restored old path plus the new outside path. The v1.14.3 replay proves the simple addition case, not this future invariant. Before restoration, inspect the full upstream-to-upstream diff with rename detection and fail for review when either side of an `R`/`C` crosses the workflow boundary.

5. **MED — “The AI cannot resurrect one” is not enforced.**  
   Removing workflow paths from the conflict list means the resolver should not touch them, but Claude still has write access to the entire tree. There is no exact postcondition before [`git add -A`](</C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/fork-superset-windows-arm64/.github/workflows/nightly-merge.yml:261>). The marker gate only proves that `(WORKFLOW-FORK-OWNED)` text remains somewhere under `.github`; retaining the comment while removing or defeating the command passes. Immediately before `git add -A`, require an empty:

   ```bash
   git status --porcelain=v1 --untracked-files=all -- .github/workflows
   ```

   After committing, also assert that the merge’s first-parent diff contains no `.github/workflows/` path.

6. **HIGH — Dropping upstream workflows creates a genuine audit blind spot.**  
   Keeping upstream YAML non-executable in this fork is the right policy, but erasing it before both conflict detection and semantic review means nobody reviews what was discarded. Upstream has previously delivered build-relevant behavior only through workflow changes:

   - `70cc2a34d`: added `generate:icons` to the desktop build.
   - `40f59ec0a`: added `app-update.yml` verification.
   - `60093e1e0`: added a required desktop build environment variable.

   I would keep the directory fork-owned but require explicit acknowledgement of every upstream workflow-tree change. Compute the full upstream-to-upstream rename-aware diff before restoration, preserve it in the job summary/artifact and semantic-review input, and fail closed until a committed acknowledgement records the reviewed upstream workflow tree OID. This catches future build logic that must be selectively ported without importing upstream CI wholesale.

7. **BLOCKER — Release tags currently target the wrong commit.**  
   [`gh release create`](</C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/fork-superset-windows-arm64/.github/workflows/build-arm64.yml:300>) runs after deleting the tag but without `--target`. When the tag is absent, `gh` creates it from the default branch—not checked-out candidate `HEAD`. During nightly, `main` has not yet advanced. Thus the installer is built from the candidate while its release tag points to the old baseline. [GitHub CLI documentation](https://cli.github.com/manual/gh_release_create).

   Repository evidence confirms this happened: local `desktop-v1.14.2` points at `13437b4a…`, whose desktop version is `1.14.0`; the verified merge `e376fbf7…` contains `1.14.2`. Use `--target "$(git rev-parse HEAD)"` and assert the resulting remote tag equals that SHA.

   Additionally, the release/tag deletions at [lines 298–299](</C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/fork-superset-windows-arm64/.github/workflows/build-arm64.yml:298>) suppress every failure. A protected or stale tag can therefore survive and be silently reused. Suppress only the expected “not found” case and verify absence/identity explicitly.

8. **HIGH — Concurrent runs can build and advance different commits.**  
   There is no workflow-level `concurrency`, and the candidate is passed around as mutable branch name `nightly/desktop-vX`. Overlapping scheduled/manual runs can delete and replace that branch between merge, build checkout, and [`advance` fetch](</C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/fork-superset-windows-arm64/.github/workflows/nightly-merge.yml:306>). Add a fixed concurrency group with `cancel-in-progress: false`, output the candidate SHA, and build/advance only that immutable SHA with equality assertions.

9. **MED — Other permission rejection paths remain.**  
   `contents: write` is sufficient for ordinary branch/tag/release operations, but it does not bypass:

   - Branch protection or rulesets on `main` or `nightly/*`.
   - Required PRs, signed commits, status checks, or linear history.
   - Tag protection/rulesets or immutable releases.

   The [`main` push](</C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/fork-superset-windows-arm64/.github/workflows/nightly-merge.yml:308>) occurs after the public release is created, so rejection leaves a published release with an unadvanced baseline. The following candidate deletion can also fail after `main` has already advanced, making a successful rollout appear failed. [Protected branch documentation](https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/configuring-branches-and-merges-in-your-repository/managing-protected-branches/about-protected-branches), [ruleset documentation](https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/configuring-branches-and-merges-in-your-repository/managing-rulesets/available-rules-for-rulesets).

10. **MED — Failure needs a durable alert.**  
    Add an always-run failure job that creates or updates one deduplicated “nightly merge blocked” issue containing the tag, failed phase, and run URL. That requires `issues: write`. A separate external watchdog is needed to detect cases where the workflow never starts because it is disabled or invalid.

## Bottom-line verdict

The shipped checkout command is sufficient for the exact v1.14.3 workflow-addition incident and normal workflow-path conflicts. Making `.github/workflows` fork-owned is preferable to introducing a PAT/deploy key merely to import unwanted upstream CI.

The pipeline as shipped is not yet sufficient overall. Before the next automatic publication, I would treat the wrong release-tag target as a blocker, then add merge-result validation, immutable candidate SHA/concurrency, and an exact no-workflow-delta assertion. For long-term vendored-fork safety, upstream workflow changes also need a separate fail-closed audit rather than being silently erased.

file.content:
[ "$fail" = 0 ] || { echo "::error::packaged native closure verification FAILED"; exit 1; }
          echo "Packaged native closure verified (ARM64)."

      - name: Resolve installer asset
        id: asset
        if: inputs.publish
        shell: bash
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          A=$(ls apps/desktop/release/*arm64*.exe 2>/dev/null | head -1 || true)
          [ -n "$A" ] || A=$(ls apps/desktop/release/*.exe 2>/dev/null | grep -v blockmap | head -1 || true)
          [ -n "$A" ] || { echo "::error::no .exe produced in apps/desktop/release/"; ls -la apps/desktop/release || true; exit 1; }
          echo "path=$A" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "Installer: $A"

      - name: Publish GitHub Release
        if: inputs.publish
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ github.token }}
        shell: bash
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          V="${{ steps.ver.outputs.version }}"
          ASSET="${{ steps.asset.outputs.path }}"
          # ONE version per upstream release: desktop-v<version>, rebuilt in
          # place. Delete + recreate so the tag always points at the latest
          # build of that version. No beta / prerelease split.
          TAG="desktop-v${V}"
          gh release delete "$TAG" --repo "${{ github.repository }}" --yes 2>/dev/null || true
          git push --delete origin "refs/tags/$TAG" 2>/dev/null || true
          gh release create "$TAG" "$ASSET" \
            --repo "${{ github.repository }}" \
            --title "Superset $V (Windows ARM64)" \
            --notes "Native **Windows ARM64** build of the vendored superset-sh/superset fork (version $V). Built deterministically from committed source (no AI in the build). Installer is **unsigned**. Not the official superset-sh distribution."

235	          while [ $attempt -le 2 ]; do

238	            echo "::endgroup::"

# --- feature-marker gate (FEATURES.md) ------------------------------
          echo "::group::feature-marker gate"
          MISSING=0
          # read the fenced ```markers block: lines are "<token><TAB><glob-root>"
          awk '/^```markers$/{f=1;next} /^```$/{f=0} f' FEATURES.md | while IFS=$'\t' read -r tok root; do
            [ -n "$tok" ] || continue
            if grep -rqsF -- "$tok" "$root" 2>/dev/null; then
              echo "  OK   $tok ($root)"
            else
              echo "::error::feature marker MISSING after merge: '$tok' under $root"
              echo missing >> "$RUNNER_TEMP/marker-missing"
            fi
          done
          echo "::endgroup::"
          if [ -f "$RUNNER_TEMP/marker-missing" ]; then
            echo "::error::A feature was dropped by the $NEWTAG merge — rejecting. Recovery: fix locally and re-baseline."
            exit 1
          fi

          # --- dependency-consistency gate + lock regen (LOCK-REGEN) ----------
          # The build installs --frozen-lockfile and electron-builder's
          # dependency traversal requires each workspace's DECLARED dep version
          # to be the one actually installed. A merge introduces two kinds of
          # drift that both kill the build ~25 min in (after a windows-arm64
          # runner spins up), not here:
          #   (1) a fork root `overrides` entry pins a package to a version
          #       DIFFERENT from the merged workspace pin (this is exactly how a
          #       stale {@mastra/core, mastracode} override broke every mastra
          #       bump: override forced 0.18.0, chat declared 0.18.1 ->
          #       electron-builder "production dependency not found 0.18.1"); and
          #   (2) the merge bumps a package.json dep but bun.lock still pins the
          #       old version (a stale frozen lock).
          # Catch (1) loud here (pure manifest analysis, platform-independent);
          # fix (2) by regenerating the lock with the build's exact bun version,
          # which ALSO fails loud if the merged manifests don't resolve or a pin
          # is age-blocked. Both run in this 2-min job, so the windows-arm64
          # build never spins up on a broken tree. We deliberately do NOT run a
          # full `--frozen-lockfile` install here: this is a linux runner, so a
          # platform-specific install/native-build quirk would be a false abort,
          # and after a successful --lockfile-only the lock is consistent with
          # the manifests by construction (same bun version the build uses).
          # Any failure below hard-aborts with the baseline untouched.
          echo "::group::dependency-consistency gate"
          node scripts/check-override-consistency.mjs
          echo "::endgroup::"

          echo "::group::regenerate bun.lock to match merged manifests"
          export SUPERSET_POSTINSTALL_RUNNING=1
          LOCKLOG="$RUNNER_TEMP/bun-lockfile.log"
          set +e
          bun install --lockfile-only > "$LOCKLOG" 2>&1
          rc=$?
          set -e
          cat "$LOCKLOG"
          if [ "$rc" -ne 0 ]; then
            if grep -q "blocked by minimum-release-age" "$LOCKLOG"; then
              echo "::error::Lock regen blocked by bunfig minimumReleaseAge — an upstream dep pin in $NEWTAG is <72h old. This is a timing race, not a breakage: the nightly will succeed once the version ages past the gate (re-run after it ages, or wait for tomorrow's schedule). Baseline left untouched."
            else
              echo "::error::bun install --lockfile-only failed (rc=$rc) after merging $NEWTAG — the merged manifests don't resolve. Recovery: fix locally and re-baseline."
            fi
            exit 1
          fi
          echo "::endgroup::"

          # --- (MERGE-SEMANTIC-GATE) AI review of the WHOLE merged delta -------
          # The marker gate only proves feature TOKENS still exist, and the
          # build's (REFERR-GATE) only catches cannot-find-name diagnostics. A
          # cleanly-merging upstream hunk can still SEMANTICALLY break a fork
          # feature with zero conflicts — v1.14.2 rebuilt the sidebar row
          # projection in a non-conflicting hunk and silently dropped the fork's
          # snooze/archive/completed/deleted fields: merged clean, compiled
          # clean, broke Snooze + Recycle Bin + archived masters. EVERY merge —
          # clean or conflicted — is therefore reviewed by Opus against
          # FEATURES.md before it may commit/build/publish. Fail CLOSED: no
          # parsable verdict (rate limit, malformed output) aborts rather than
          # ships an unreviewed merge; a BREAKAGE verdict hard-aborts with the
          # findings in the log (baseline untouched -> recovery loop).
          echo "::group::semantic feature review (Opus)"
          npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
          REVIEW_FILE="$RUNNER_TEMP/merge-review.txt"
          rm -f "$REVIEW_FILE"
          # The reviewer must not modify the tree; snapshot the work-tree state
          # and refuse to continue if the review run changed ANYTHING.
          STATUS_BEFORE=$(git status --porcelain=v1 | sha256sum)
          REVIEW_PROMPT="IMPORTANT: Do NOT enter plan mode. You are reviewing an ALREADY-RESOLVED, staged, uncommitted merge of upstream superset tag ${NEWTAG} into our Windows-ARM64 fork. Your ONLY job is to decide whether the merged result SEMANTICALLY breaks any fork feature. Steps: (1) Read FEATURES.md — the fork feature manifest (feature descriptions + marker tokens). (2) Run 'git --no-pager diff --cached --name-only' to list every file the merge changed. (3) For each changed file that participates in a fork feature — it contains a FEATURES.md marker token, or it is in the blast radius of one (e.g. a projection/select/mapping that a feature's fields flow through, a component a feature's props pass through, a hook a feature's guard lives in) — read the staged diff ('git --no-pager diff --cached -- <file>') AND the surrounding file content, and verify the fork behaviour still works end-to-end: fork-added fields still projected/carried, fork branches and guards still reachable, fork props still passed, fork call sites still invoked. An upstream refactor that preserves behaviour is fine; flag only real breakage. (4) Write your verdict to the file at ${REVIEW_FILE} — the FIRST line must be exactly 'VERDICT: OK' or 'VERDICT: BREAKAGE'; for BREAKAGE add one line per finding: '<file> — <feature/marker> — <what broke>'. Rules: do NOT edit ANY repository file; do NOT run git add / commit / checkout / merge / restore; only read files, run read-only git diff/log/show commands, and write the verdict file. Output nothing else."
          attempt=1
          while [ $attempt -le 2 ]; do
            echo "::group::claude review attempt $attempt"
            claude --dangerously-skip-permissions --model opus --effort high -p "$REVIEW_PROMPT" || true
            echo "::endgroup::"
            if [ -s "$REVIEW_FILE" ] && head -1 "$REVIEW_FILE" | grep -qE '^VERDICT: (OK|BREAKAGE)$'; then break; fi
            attempt=$((attempt+1))
          done
          STATUS_AFTER=$(git status --porcelain=v1 | sha256sum)
          if [ "$STATUS_BEFORE" != "$STATUS_AFTER" ]; then
            echo "::error::(MERGE-SEMANTIC-GATE) the review run modified the work tree — refusing to trust or commit it. Baseline untouched."
            exit 1
          fi
          if ! [ -s "$REVIEW_FILE" ] || ! head -1 "$REVIEW_FILE" | grep -qE '^VERDICT: (OK|BREAKAGE)$'; then
            echo "::error::(MERGE-SEMANTIC-GATE) no parsable review verdict for $NEWTAG — refusing to ship an UNREVIEWED merge (fail closed). Baseline untouched."
            exit 1
          fi
          echo "--- review verdict ---"
          cat "$REVIEW_FILE"
          if head -1 "$REVIEW_FILE" | grep -q 'BREAKAGE'; then
            echo "::error::(MERGE-SEMANTIC-GATE) Opus found fork-feature breakage in the $NEWTAG merge — rejecting. Findings above. Recovery: fix locally and re-baseline."
            exit 1
          fi
          echo "::endgroup::"

          # --- record new baseline + commit the merge -------------------------
          printf '%s\n' "$NEWTAG" > .fork/upstream-baseline.txt
          git add -A
          git commit -m "merge upstream ${NEWTAG}" -m "Auto-merged by nightly-merge; conflicts resolved by Opus where present; all FEATURES.md markers verified present; merged delta passed the (MERGE-SEMANTIC-GATE) Opus feature review."
          # (CANDIDATE-IDEMPOTENT-PUSH) The candidate is a disposable CI branch:
          # re-derived from main each run, consumed by `build`, deleted by
          # `advance` ON SUCCESS only. A FAILED build never reaches `advance`, so
          # it strands the candidate on the remote. The next night re-merges the
          # SAME upstream version into a NEW commit (AI conflict resolution is
          # non-deterministic AND main may have advanced), whose history diverges
          # from the stale branch — a plain push is then rejected non-fast-forward
          # and EVERY subsequent nightly deadlocks on that stale ref. Drop any
          # stale remote copy first so the push is always clean. This is pure CI
          # scratch (never a feature/history branch), so overwriting it is safe;
          # we delete-then-push rather than --force a real branch.
          git push origin --delete "$CAND" 2>/dev/null || true
          git push origin "$CAND"

          VERSION=$(node -p "require('./apps/desktop/package.json').version")
          echo "should_build=true" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "newtag=$NEWTAG" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "version=$VERSION" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "candidate=$CAND" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "Merged $NEWTAG -> candidate $CAND (version $VERSION). Building next."

  build:
    needs: merge
    if: needs.merge.outputs.should_build == 'true'
    uses: ./.github/workflows/build-arm64.yml
    with:
      ref: ${{ needs.merge.outputs.candidate }}
      publish: true

  advance:
    needs: [merge, build]
    if: needs.merge.outputs.should_build == 'true'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v5
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0
      - name: Fast-forward main to the verified candidate + clean up
        shell: bash
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          CAND='${{ needs.merge.outputs.candidate }}'
          git fetch origin "$CAND"
          # The candidate is main + the merge commit, so this is a fast-forward.
          git push origin FETCH_HEAD:refs/heads/main
          git push origin --delete "$CAND"
          echo "Baseline advanced to ${{ needs.merge.outputs.newtag }}; Release published by the build job."

content.content:
1	name: Build Windows ARM64 (vendored fork)
2	
3	# Deterministic, NO-AI build of the vendored fork source at repo root.
4	# Unlike the legacy pipeline there is NO upstream clone, NO patch apply, NO AI
5	# re-anchor and NO arch-fixup: every Windows/ARM64 change already lives in the
6	# committed source (the fork tree). This workflow only: install -> build ->
7	# materialize the win-arm64 native closure -> package the NSIS installer ->
8	# verify the packaged natives are ARM64 -> (optionally) publish a release.
9	#
10	# It is the build half of the architecture: the baseline is frozen in this repo;
11	# nightly-merge.yml carries upstream deltas in and calls a build like this to
12	# prove the result before publishing.
13	
14	on:
15	  workflow_dispatch:
16	    inputs:
17	      publish:
18	        description: 'Publish the desktop-v<version> Release (rebuilt in place; one version, no beta)'
19	        type: boolean
20	        default: true
21	  # Reusable so nightly-merge.yml can invoke the exact same build.
22	  workflow_call:
23	    inputs:
24	      publish:
25	        type: boolean
26	        default: false
27	      ref:
28	        description: 'Git ref to build (default: the triggering ref)'
29	        type: string
30	        default: ''
31	
32	permissions:
33	  contents: write
34	
35	jobs:
36	  build:
37	    runs-on: windows-11-arm
38	    steps:
39	      # Superset has paths deeper than Windows' 260-char MAX_PATH; without this
40	      # actions/checkout dies with "Filename too long". --system so the
41	      # checkout action's own git invocation picks it up.
42	      - name: Configure git long paths (Windows MAX_PATH)
43	        shell: bash
44	        run: git config --system core.longpaths true || git config --global core.longpaths true
45	
46	      - name: Checkout fork source
47	        uses: actions/checkout@v5
48	        with:
49	          ref: ${{ inputs.ref || github.ref }}
50	          fetch-depth: 1
51	
52	      - name: Read app version
53	        id: ver
54	        shell: bash
55	        run: |
56	          V=$(node -p "require('./apps/desktop/package.json').version")
57	          [ -n "$V" ] || { echo "::error::could not read apps/desktop/package.json version"; exit 1; }
58	          echo "version=$V" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
59	          echo "App version: $V"
60	
61	      - name: Setup Node.js
62	        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
63	        with:
64	          node-version: '22'
65	
66	      - name: Setup Bun (native Windows ARM64)
67	        uses: oven-sh/setup-bun@v2
68	        with:
69	          # 1.3.14 = last stable Zig release; first native win-arm64 shipped in 1.3.10.
70	          bun-version: '1.3.14'
71	
72	      - name: Verify Bun is native ARM64 (fail fast, fall back to official zip)
73	        shell: pwsh
74	        run: |
75	          function Get-PEMachine($p){ $fs=[IO.File]::OpenRead($p); $br=New-Object IO.BinaryReader($fs); $fs.Position=0x3C; $pe=$br.ReadInt32(); $fs.Position=$pe+4; $m=$br.ReadUInt16(); $br.Close(); return $m }

file.content:
name: Build Windows ARM64 (vendored fork)

# Deterministic, NO-AI build of the vendored fork source at repo root.
# Unlike the legacy pipeline there is NO upstream clone, NO patch apply, NO AI
# re-anchor and NO arch-fixup: every Windows/ARM64 change already lives in the
# committed source (the fork tree). This workflow only: install -> build ->
# materialize the win-arm64 native closure -> package the NSIS installer ->
# verify the packaged natives are ARM64 -> (optionally) publish a release.
#
# It is the build half of the architecture: the baseline is frozen in this repo;
# nightly-merge.yml carries upstream deltas in and calls a build like this to
# prove the result before publishing.

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      publish:
        description: 'Publish the desktop-v<version> Release (rebuilt in place; one version, no beta)'
        type: boolean
        default: true
  # Reusable so nightly-merge.yml can invoke the exact same build.
  workflow_call:
    inputs:
      publish:
        type: boolean
        default: false
      ref:
        description: 'Git ref to build (default: the triggering ref)'
        type: string
        default: ''

permissions:
  contents: write

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: windows-11-arm
    steps:
      # Superset has paths deeper than Windows' 260-char MAX_PATH; without this
      # actions/checkout dies with "Filename too long". --system so the
      # checkout action's own git invocation picks it up.
      - name: Configure git long paths (Windows MAX_PATH)
        shell: bash
        run: git config --system core.longpaths true || git config --global core.longpaths true

      - name: Checkout fork source
        uses: actions/checkout@v5
        with:
          ref: ${{ inputs.ref || github.ref }}
          fetch-depth: 1

      - name: Read app version
        id: ver
        shell: bash
        run: |
          V=$(node -p "require('./apps/desktop/package.json').version")
          [ -n "$V" ] || { echo "::error::could not read apps/desktop/package.json version"; exit 1; }
          echo "version=$V" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "App version: $V"

      - name: Setup Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '22'

      - name: Setup Bun (native Windows ARM64)
        uses: oven-sh/setup-bun@v2
        with:
          # 1.3.14 = last stable Zig release; first native win-arm64 shipped in 1.3.10.
          bun-version: '1.3.14'

      - name: Verify Bun is native ARM64 (fail fast, fall back to official zip)
        shell: pwsh
        run: |
          function Get-PEMachine($p){ $fs=[IO.File]::OpenRead($p); $br=New-Object IO.BinaryReader($fs); $fs.Position=0x3C; $pe=$br.ReadInt32(); $fs.Position=$pe+4; $m=$br.ReadUInt16(); $br.Close(); return $m }

content.text: `ref` feeds only checkout — SHA-safe. Applying edits:

input.new_string:
permissions:
  contents: write

# (NIGHTLY-INTEGRITY) Never let two nightly instances interleave: an overlapping
# schedule + manual dispatch can delete/replace the mutable candidate branch
# between one run's merge, build checkout and advance. Queue, never cancel — a
# running merge/build must finish or fail on its own.
concurrency:
  group: nightly-merge
  cancel-in-progress: false

jobs:
  merge:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      should_build: ${{ steps.do.outputs.should_build }}
      newtag: ${{ steps.do.outputs.newtag }}
      version: ${{ steps.do.outputs.version }}
      candidate: ${{ steps.do.outputs.candidate }}
      sha: ${{ steps.do.outputs.sha }}

input.old_string:
set +e
          git merge --no-commit --no-ff "refs/upstream-tags/$NEWTAG^{commit}"
          set -e

          # (WORKFLOW-FORK-OWNED) .github/workflows is FORK-OWNED: the fork
input.new_string:
set +e
          git merge --no-commit --no-ff "refs/upstream-tags/$NEWTAG^{commit}"
          set -e

          # (NIGHTLY-INTEGRITY) `set +e` above accepts EVERY nonzero merge exit,
          # not just "conflicts". A fatal merge failure (lock error, corrupt
          # object, OOM) leaves NO MERGE_HEAD and possibly no conflicts either —
          # without this check it would read as a clean merge and we'd commit a
          # single-parent non-merge with a bumped baseline.
          if ! git rev-parse -q --verify MERGE_HEAD >/dev/null; then
            echo "::error::git merge of $NEWTAG failed fatally (no MERGE_HEAD) — aborting. Baseline untouched."
            exit 1
          fi

          # (WORKFLOW-FORK-OWNED) Surface — never silently discard — upstream's
          # workflow-tree delta before we drop it: upstream sometimes moves
          # build-relevant logic INTO CI (a codegen step, a required env var).
          # The delta goes to the job summary + log and is handed to the
          # semantic review below so build-relevant CI changes get flagged.
          WFDELTA_FILE="$RUNNER_TEMP/upstream-workflow-delta.diff"
          : > "$WFDELTA_FILE"
          if git rev-parse -q --verify "refs/upstream-tags/$BASE^{commit}" >/dev/null; then
            git diff "refs/upstream-tags/$BASE" "refs/upstream-tags/$NEWTAG" -- .github/workflows > "$WFDELTA_FILE" || true
          fi
          if [ -s "$WFDELTA_FILE" ]; then
            {
              echo "## Upstream workflow-tree delta ($BASE → $NEWTAG) — DROPPED, .github/workflows is fork-owned"
              echo '```'
              git diff --name-status "refs/upstream-tags/$BASE" "refs/upstream-tags/$NEWTAG" -- .github/workflows
              echo '```'
              echo "Full patch in the job log (group: dropped upstream workflow delta). Port anything build-relevant by hand."
            } >> "$GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY"
            echo "::group::dropped upstream workflow delta"
            cat "$WFDELTA_FILE"
            echo "::endgroup::"
          fi

          # (WORKFLOW-FORK-OWNED) .github/workflows is FORK-OWNED: the fork

input.old_string: (4) Write your verdict to the file at ${REVIEW_FILE} — the FIRST line must be exactly 'VERDICT: OK' or 'VERDICT: BREAKAGE'; for BREAKAGE add one line per finding: '<file> — <feature/marker> — <what broke>'. Rules: do NOT edit ANY repository file;
input.new_string: (4) Write your verdict to the file at ${REVIEW_FILE} — the FIRST line must be exactly 'VERDICT: OK' or 'VERDICT: BREAKAGE'; for BREAKAGE add one line per finding: '<file> — <feature/marker> — <what broke>'. (5) If the file at ${WFDELTA_FILE} is non-empty, read it — it is upstream's CI/workflow delta we deliberately did NOT merge (.github/workflows is fork-owned). If it moves BUILD-RELEVANT logic into upstream CI (a codegen/generate step, a newly required env var, a packaging/signing step) that our fork's own build workflow would now silently miss, append one line per item after the verdict: 'CI-NOTE: <what upstream CI now does that the fork build may need>'. CI-NOTEs alone never change the verdict. Rules: do NOT edit ANY repository file;

lines: # The reviewer must not modify the tree; snapshot the work-tree state
# and refuse to continue if the review run changed ANYTHING.

-          REVIEW_PROMPT="IMPORTANT: Do NOT enter plan mode. You are reviewing an ALREADY-RESOLVED, staged, uncommitted merge of upstream superset tag ${NEWTAG} into our Windows-ARM64 fork. Your ONLY job is to decide whether the merged result SEMANTICALLY breaks any fork feature. Steps: (1) Read FEATURES.md — the fork feature manifest (feature descriptions + marker tokens). (2) Run 'git --no-pager diff --cached --name-only' to list every file the merge changed. (3) For each changed file that participates in a fork feature — it contains a FEATURES.md marker token, or it is in the blast radius of one (e.g. a projection/select/mapping that a feature's fields flow through, a component a feature's props pass through, a hook a feature's guard lives in) — read the staged diff ('git --no-pager diff --cached -- <file>') AND the surrounding file content, and verify the fork behaviour still works end-to-end: fork-added fields still projected/carried, fork branches and guards still reachable, fork props still passed, fork call sites still invoked. An upstream refactor that preserves behaviour is fine; flag only real breakage. (4) Write your verdict to the file at ${REVIEW_FILE} — the FIRST line must be exactly 'VERDICT: OK' or 'VERDICT: BREAKAGE'; for BREAKAGE add one line per finding: '<file> — <feature/marker> — <what broke>'. Rules: do NOT edit ANY repository file; do NOT run git add / commit / checkout / merge / restore; only read files, run read-only git diff/log/show commands, and write the verdict file. Output nothing else."

+          REVIEW_PROMPT="IMPORTANT: Do NOT enter plan mode. You are reviewing an ALREADY-RESOLVED, staged, uncommitted merge of upstream superset tag ${NEWTAG} into our Windows-ARM64 fork. Your ONLY job is to decide whether the merged result SEMANTICALLY breaks any fork feature. Steps: (1) Read FEATURES.md — the fork feature manifest (feature descriptions + marker tokens). (2) Run 'git --no-pager diff --cached --name-only' to list every file the merge changed. (3) For each changed file that participates in a fork feature — it contains a FEATURES.md marker token, or it is in the blast radius of one (e.g. a projection/select/mapping that a feature's fields flow through, a component a feature's props pass through, a hook a feature's guard lives in) — read the staged diff ('git --no-pager diff --cached -- <file>') AND the surrounding file content, and verify the fork behaviour still works end-to-end: fork-added fields still projected/carried, fork branches and guards still reachable, fork props still passed, fork call sites still invoked. An upstream refactor that preserves behaviour is fine; flag only real breakage. (4) Write your verdict to the file at ${REVIEW_FILE} — the FIRST line must be exactly 'VERDICT: OK' or 'VERDICT: BREAKAGE'; for BREAKAGE add one line per finding: '<file> — <feature/marker> — <what broke>'. (5) If the file at ${WFDELTA_FILE} is non-empty, read it — it is upstream's CI/workflow delta we deliberately did NOT merge (.github/workflows is fork-owned). If it moves BUILD-RELEVANT logic into upstream CI (a codegen/generate step, a newly required env var, a packaging/signing step) that our fork's own build workflow would now silently miss, append one line per item after the verdict: 'CI-NOTE: <what upstream CI now does that the fork build may need>'. CI-NOTEs alone never change the verdict. Rules: do NOT edit ANY repository file; do NOT run git add / commit / checkout / merge / restore; only read files, run read-only git diff/log/show commands, and write the verdict file. Output nothing else."

input.old_string:
# --- record new baseline + commit the merge -------------------------
          printf '%s\n' "$NEWTAG" > .fork/upstream-baseline.txt
input.new_string:
# --- record new baseline + commit the merge -------------------------
          # (WORKFLOW-FORK-OWNED) postcondition: the tree about to be committed
          # must contain ZERO workflow-dir changes — not from the merge, not
          # from the AI resolver, not from the review run. The restore step
          # above is the mechanism; this is the proof (a workflow delta would be
          # rejected by the push anyway — fail here, loud, with the baseline
          # untouched).
          if [ -n "$(git status --porcelain=v1 --untracked-files=all -- .github/workflows)" ]; then
            echo "::error::(WORKFLOW-FORK-OWNED) .github/workflows differs from HEAD after resolve/review — refusing to commit:"
            git status --porcelain=v1 --untracked-files=all -- .github/workflows
            exit 1
          fi
          printf '%s\n' "$NEWTAG" > .fork/upstream-baseline.txt

git commit -m "merge upstream ${NEWTAG}" -m "Auto-merged by nightly-merge; conflicts resolved by Opus where present; all FEATURES.md markers verified present; merged delta passed the (MERGE-SEMANTIC-GATE) Opus feature review."

input.old_string:
VERSION=$(node -p "require('./apps/desktop/package.json').version")
          echo "should_build=true" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "newtag=$NEWTAG" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "version=$VERSION" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "candidate=$CAND" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "Merged $NEWTAG -> candidate $CAND (version $VERSION). Building next."

  build:
    needs: merge
    if: needs.merge.outputs.should_build == 'true'
    uses: ./.github/workflows/build-arm64.yml
    with:
      ref: ${{ needs.merge.outputs.candidate }}
      publish: true

  advance:
    needs: [merge, build]
    if: needs.merge.outputs.should_build == 'true'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v5
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0
      - name: Fast-forward main to the verified candidate + clean up
        shell: bash
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          CAND='${{ needs.merge.outputs.candidate }}'
          git fetch origin "$CAND"
          # The candidate is main + the merge commit, so this is a fast-forward.
          git push origin FETCH_HEAD:refs/heads/main
          git push origin --delete "$CAND"
          echo "Baseline advanced to ${{ needs.merge.outputs.newtag }}; Release published by the build job."
input.new_string:
VERSION=$(node -p "require('./apps/desktop/package.json').version")
          echo "should_build=true" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "newtag=$NEWTAG" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "version=$VERSION" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "candidate=$CAND" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          # (NIGHTLY-INTEGRITY) build + advance consume this immutable SHA, not
          # the mutable branch name — a replaced candidate can't be built or
          # advanced unnoticed.
          echo "sha=$(git rev-parse HEAD)" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "Merged $NEWTAG -> candidate $CAND @ $(git rev-parse HEAD) (version $VERSION). Building next."

  build:
    needs: merge
    if: needs.merge.outputs.should_build == 'true'
    uses: ./.github/workflows/build-arm64.yml
    with:
      # (NIGHTLY-INTEGRITY) build the exact merge commit, immune to candidate-
      # branch mutation between jobs.
      ref: ${{ needs.merge.outputs.sha }}
      publish: true

  advance:
    needs: [merge, build]
    if: needs.merge.outputs.should_build == 'true'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v5
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0
      - name: Fast-forward main to the verified candidate + clean up
        shell: bash
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          CAND='${{ needs.merge.outputs.candidate }}'
          SHA='${{ needs.merge.outputs.sha }}'
          git fetch origin "$CAND"
          # (NIGHTLY-INTEGRITY) advance ONLY the commit that was gated + built.
          if [ "$(git rev-parse FETCH_HEAD)" != "$SHA" ]; then
            echo "::error::candidate $CAND moved (expected $SHA, found $(git rev-parse FETCH_HEAD)) — another run interfered; refusing to advance main."
            exit 1
          fi
          # The candidate is main + the merge commit, so this is a fast-forward.
          git push origin "$SHA:refs/heads/main"
          git push origin --delete "$CAND"
          echo "Baseline advanced to ${{ needs.merge.outputs.newtag }}; Release published by the build job."

      # branch mutation between jobs.
      ref: ${{ needs.merge.outputs.sha }}
      publish: true

  advance:
    needs: [merge, build]
    if: needs.merge.outputs.should_build == 'true'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v5
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0
      - name: Fast-forward main to the verified candidate + clean up
        shell: bash
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          CAND='${{ needs.merge.outputs.candidate }}'
          SHA='${{ needs.merge.outputs.sha }}'
          git fetch origin "$CAND"
          # (NIGHTLY-INTEGRITY) advance ONLY the commit that was gated + built.
          if [ "$(git rev-parse FETCH_HEAD)" != "$SHA" ]; then
            echo "::error::candidate $CAND moved (expected $SHA, found $(git rev-parse FETCH_HEAD)) — another run interfered; refusing to advance main."
            exit 1
          fi
          # The candidate is main + the merge commit, so this is a fast-forward.
          git push origin "$SHA:refs/heads/main"
          git push origin --delete "$CAND"
          echo "Baseline advanced to ${{ needs.merge.outputs.newtag }}; Release published by the build job."

content.text: Now build-arm64.yml release tag fix:

input.old_string:
TAG="desktop-v${V}"
          gh release delete "$TAG" --repo "${{ github.repository }}" --yes 2>/dev/null || true
          git push --delete origin "refs/tags/$TAG" 2>/dev/null || true
          gh release create "$TAG" "$ASSET" \
            --repo "${{ github.repository }}" \
            --title "Superset $V (Windows ARM64)" \
            --notes "Native **Windows ARM64** build of the vendored superset-sh/superset fork (version $V). Built deterministically from committed source (no AI in the build). Installer is **unsigned**. Not the official superset-sh distribution."
input.new_string:
TAG="desktop-v${V}"
          gh release delete "$TAG" --repo "${{ github.repository }}" --yes 2>/dev/null || true
          git push --delete origin "refs/tags/$TAG" 2>/dev/null || true
          # (NIGHTLY-INTEGRITY) --target: without it gh creates the tag at the
          # DEFAULT BRANCH tip — which during a nightly is main BEFORE advance
          # runs, so every nightly-published tag pointed one commit behind the
          # candidate the installer was actually built from. Tag the exact
          # checked-out commit, then prove the remote tag landed on it.
          BUILT_SHA=$(git rev-parse HEAD)
          gh release create "$TAG" "$ASSET" \
            --repo "${{ github.repository }}" \
            --target "$BUILT_SHA" \
            --title "Superset $V (Windows ARM64)" \
            --notes "Native **Windows ARM64** build of the vendored superset-sh/superset fork (version $V). Built deterministically from committed source (no AI in the build). Installer is **unsigned**. Not the official superset-sh distribution."
          REMOTE_TAG_SHA=$(git ls-remote origin "refs/tags/$TAG" | cut -f1)
          if [ "$REMOTE_TAG_SHA" != "$BUILT_SHA" ]; then
            echo "::error::release tag $TAG points at ${REMOTE_TAG_SHA:-<missing>}, expected built commit $BUILT_SHA"
            exit 1
          fi
          echo "Release $TAG published at $BUILT_SHA."
